blocks.text: In the assembly of the Most High she opens her mouth, and in the presence of his hosts she tells of her glory:
1–2 : , Israel; , the heavenly host or divine council (cf. Ps 82.1,6–7).
“I came forth from the mouth of the Most High, and covered the earth like a mist.
3 : evokes Gen 1. , cf. Gen 1.2; Wis 7.25.
I dwelt in the highest heavens, and my throne was in a pillar of cloud.
Alone I compassed the vault of heaven and traversed the depths of the abyss.
Over waves of the sea, over all the earth, and over every people and nation I have held sway.
6 : Wisdom has a universal reach (cf. 1.15).
Among all these I sought a resting place; in whose territory should I abide?
“Then the Creator of all things gave me a command, and my Creator chose the place for my tent. He said, ‘Make your dwelling in Jacob, and in Israel receive your inheritance.’
Before the ages, in the beginning, he created me, and for all the ages I shall not cease to be.
In the holy tent I ministered before him, and so I was established in Zion.
10 : , an allusion to the tabernacle (Ex 25.8–9), which represents the Temple. , Wisdom functions liturgically as a priest.
Thus in the beloved city he gave me a resting place, and in Jerusalem was my domain.
I took root in an honored people, in the portion of the Lord, his heritage.
8–12 : Wisdom, who was created (cf. Gen 1.1), dwells with Israel (cf. Bar 3.36–37) in the Jerusalem Temple.
“I grew tall like a cedar in Lebanon, and like a cypress on the heights of Hermon.
13 : , a mountain range in modern Lebanon and Syria famous for its forests. , a mountain on the border of modern Israel and Syria.
I grew tall like a palm tree in En-gedi, and like rosebushes in Jericho; like a fair olive tree in the field, and like a plane tree beside water I grew tall.
14 : and , oases in the region near the Dead Sea.
Like cassia and camel's thorn I gave forth perfume, and like choice myrrh I spread my fragrance, like galbanum, onycha, and stacte, and like the odor of incense in the tent.
15 : These ingredients were used in the service of the Temple (Ex 30.22–38), which is infused with Wisdom's presence, just as with .
Like a terebinth I spread out my branches, and my branches are glorious and graceful.
Like the vine I bud forth delights, and my blossoms become glorious and abundant fruit.

13–17 : A comparison of Wisdom with plants, , and fragrant substances, emphasizing her beauty and expanse and her connection with the Temple. See 50.8–12 where , , , , and describe the high priest Simon II.
“Come to me, you who desire me, and eat your fill of my fruits.
For the memory of me is sweeter than honey, and the possession of me sweeter than the honeycomb.
Those who eat of me will hunger for more, and those who drink of me will thirst for more.
Whoever obeys me will not be put to shame, and those who work with me will not sin.”

24.1–22. Wisdom's self-praise. An aretalogy (first-person praise of oneself, often connected with the Egyptian goddess Isis) in which Wisdom is personified as a woman (Prov 8.22–36; cf. Bar 3.9–4.4; Wis 7.22–8.1) and dwells in the Jerusalem Temple.
19–22 : Wisdom is better than fine food and drink, and those who and will desire more (cf. 6.19; Prov 9.1–6).
All this is the book of the covenant of the Most High God, the law that Moses commanded us as an inheritance for the congregations of Jacob.
23 : Ben Sira is the first to relate Torah and wisdom in this way; see also Bar 4.1.
23 : The (cf. Ex 24.7; Deut 33.4) embodies the eternal, timeless Wisdom of vv. 3–22 (cf. Deut 4.5–8), an important statement about the confluence of the Torah and the wisdom traditions.
It overflows, like the Pishon, with wisdom, and like the Tigris at the time of the first fruits.
It runs over, like the Euphrates, with understanding, and like the Jordan at harvest time.
It pours forth instruction like the Nile, like the Gihon at the time of vintage.
25–27 : The , , , and flow from the Garden of Eden (Gen 2.10–14).
The first man did not know wisdom fully, nor will the last one fathom her.
For her thoughts are more abundant than the sea, and her counsel deeper than the great abyss.
28–29 : Wisdom will never be fully grasped, not from to the ; cf. 1.4–8.
As for me, I was like a canal from a river, like a water channel into a garden.
I said, “I will water my garden and drench my flower-beds.” And lo, my canal became a river, and my river a sea.
I will again make instruction shine forth like the dawn, and I will make it clear from far away.
I will again pour out teaching like prophecy, and leave it to all future generations.
33 : , Ben Sira's teaching has a revelatory quality to it.
Observe that I have not labored for myself alone, but for all who seek wisdom.c
24.24–34: Ben Sira's interpretation.
30–34 : Ben Sira connects his own teaching to both Torah and Wisdom through the metaphor of a , , , and .
